title: Puppy Stuck in Birth Canal: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If a puppy is stuck halfway out of the birth canal, this is a critical veterinary emergency. Learn what to do and why immediate care is necessary.
content_html:
TL;DR: A puppy stuck in the birth canal is a life-threatening emergency known as dystocia that requires immediate veterinary care. Contact an emergency clinic right away to prevent stillbirth or life-threatening complications for the mother.

What is dystocia and why is my puppy stuck during birth?

Dystocia is the medical term for a difficult birth. A puppy may become stuck for several reasons:

  • The puppy is too large to pass through the birth canal.
  • The puppy is in an abnormal position, such as breech with legs caught.
  • The mother's uterine contractions are not strong enough to complete the delivery.

Is it an emergency if my dog has a puppy stuck in the birth canal?

Yes, this is a High-Priority Emergency. Professional intervention is required because:

  • Pressure on the umbilical cord can cut off oxygen within minutes, leading to stillbirth.
  • A stuck puppy can cause life-threatening trauma or infection for the mother.
  • You must contact an emergency veterinarian or your local clinic immediately for assistance.

How does sending a photo help the veterinarian triage a stuck puppy?

  • Taking a clear photo of the puppy's position allows the veterinary team to assess the situation while you are en route.
  • Photos help the team determine if the puppy can be manually assisted or if they need to prepare for an immediate emergency Cesarean section (C-section).
  • Only take a photo if it can be done safely without delaying your trip to the emergency vet.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Dystocia should be suspected if parturition does not occur within 24 hours after a drop in rectal temperature to below 100°F (37.7°C), strong abdominal contractions persist for 1-2 hours without passage of a puppy, active labor lasts for 1-2 hours without delivery of subsequent puppies, a resting period during active labor exceeds 4-6 hours, the bitch is in obvious pain, or abnormal vulvar discharge is present (e.g., frank blood, dark green discharge before any neonates are born, indicating placental separation). A sterile digital vaginal examination should be performed to evaluate patency of the birth canal, fetal position, and presentation. Radiography or ultrasonography can determine the presence, number, size, position, and viability of fetuses. Medical management may be considered when the dam and fetuses are stable, there is proper fetal position and presentation, and no obstruction is present.

Chapter: Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1395)

faqs.question: Can I pull the puppy out myself?
faqs.answer: You should never pull forcefully on a stuck puppy. If a vet instructs you over the phone, you may be told to apply very gentle traction with a clean towel only during a contraction, but improper technique can cause fatal internal damage to the mother or the puppy.

faqs.question: How long can a puppy stay stuck before it is a problem?
faqs.answer: Any puppy that has not been fully delivered within 10 to 15 minutes of first appearing is in critical danger. Immediate veterinary intervention is required to save the puppy and protect the mother.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the mother as calm and comfortable as possible. Keep her on a clean, soft surface and try to prevent her from sitting directly on the stuck puppy. Have someone call the vet ahead of time so they are ready for your arrival.
